The people had, by sins, made themselves ripe for ruin; and their glory was as quickly cut down and taken away by the enemy, as the corn is out of the field by the husbandman. Mercy is reserved in the midst of judgment, for a remnant. But very few shall be marked to be saved. Only here and there one was left behind. But they shall be a remnant made holy. The few that are saved were awakened to return to God. They shall acknowledge his hand in all events; they shall give him the glory due to his name. To bring us to this, is the design of his providence, as he is our Maker; and the work of his grace, as he is the Holy One of Israel. They shall look off from their idols, the creatures of their own fancy. We have reason to account those afflictions happy, which part between us and our sins. The God of our salvation is the Rock of our strength; and our forgetfulness and unmindfulness of him are at the bottom of all sin. The pleasant plants, and shoots from a foreign soil, are expressions for strange and idolatrous worship, and the vile practices connected therewith. Diligence would be used to promote the growth of these strange slips, but all in vain. See the evil and danger of sin, and its certain consequences.
17.m: Sin ruins cities. It’s odd that powerful leaders take pride in hurting people, but it’s better if animals live there instead of rebels against God. The strongholds of Israel, the kingdom of the ten tribes, will be destroyed. People who share in sin suffer together. The people sinned so much that they deserved punishment, and their glory was quickly taken away by the enemy, just like crops are harvested from fields. Even in punishment, some mercy remains. Only a few will be saved. Just a small number will survive. These survivors will become holy. A few people will wake up and turn back to God. They will see God’s hand in everything and praise him. This is what God wants through his actions as our Creator and his grace as the Holy One of Israel. They will stop following false gods they created. We should consider blessings the times when we’re separated from our sins. Our God, who saves us, is our rock of strength. Forgetting him leads to sin. Foreign plants and shoots represent strange and idolatrous worship and bad behavior. Efforts to grow these foreign plants will fail. Sin has terrible results and dangers.

17.o: The rage and force of the Assyrians resembled the mighty waters of the sea; but when the God of Israel should rebuke them, they would flee like chaff, or like a rolling thing, before the whirlwind. In the evening Jerusalem would be in trouble, because of the powerful invader, but before morning his army would be nearly cut off. Happy are those who remember God as their salvation, and rely on his power and grace. The trouble of the believers, and the prosperity of their enemies, will be equally short; while the joy of the former, and the destruction of those that hate and spoil them, shall last for ever.
17.m: The strength of the Assyrians was like the strong waves of the sea. But when the God of Israel spoke to them, they would run away like chaff in the wind. At night, Jerusalem would face trouble from the enemy. But by morning, most of the enemy’s army would be gone. Those who trust God for safety are happy. The troubles of believers and the success of their enemies both don’t last long. But the joy of the faithful lasts forever, while those who harm them will be destroyed forever.

19.o: God shall come into Egypt with his judgments. He will raise up the causes of their destruction from among themselves. When ungodly men escape danger, they are apt to think themselves secure; but evil pursues sinners, and will speedily overtake them, except they repent. The Egyptians will be given over into the hand of one who shall rule them with rigour, as was shortly after fulfilled. The Egyptians were renowned for wisdom and science; yet the Lord would give them up to their own perverse schemes, and to quarrel, till their land would be brought by their contests to become an object of contempt and pity. He renders sinners afraid of those whom they have despised and oppressed; and the Lord of hosts will make the workers of iniquity a terror to themselves, and to each other; and every object around a terror to them.
19.m:
God will bring judgment on Egypt. He will cause their ruin to come from within. Ungodly people often feel safe if they avoid trouble, but sin catches up with them unless they turn away from it. 

The Egyptians will be handed over to someone who will rule them harshly, which came true soon after. They were known for being wise and skilled, yet God let them follow foolish plans until their fights made their country look weak and pitiful.

Sinners fear those they once looked down on. The Lord makes wicked people scared of each other and everything around them.

19.o: The words, "In that day," do not always refer to the passage just before. At a time which was to come, the Egyptians shall speak the holy language, the Scripture language; not only understand it, but use it. Converting grace, by changing the heart, changes the language; for out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks. So many Jews shall come to Egypt, that they shall soon fill five cities. Where the sun was worshipped, a place infamous for idolatry, even there shall be a wonderful reformation. Christ, the great Altar, who sanctifies every gift, shall be owned, and the gospel sacrifices of prayer and praise shall be offered up. Let the broken-hearted and afflicted, whom the Lord has wounded, and thus taught to return to, and call upon him, take courage; for He will heal their souls, and turn their sorrowing supplications into joyful praises. The Gentile nations shall not only unite with each other in the gospel fold under Christ, the great Shepherd, but they shall all be united with the Jews. They shall be owned together by him; they shall all share in one and the same blessing. Meeting at the same throne of grace, and serving with each other in the same business of religion, should end all disputes, and unite the hearts of believers to each other in holy love.
19.m:
"In that day" doesn't always mean right after what came before. In a future time, the people in Egypt will start speaking the holy language, the language used in the Scriptures. They won’t just understand it, but they’ll use it too. When God’s grace changes someone’s heart, it also changes how they talk. What comes from the heart shows through our words.

Many Jewish people will move to Egypt, filling five whole cities. Places where people once worshiped the sun and idols will now see big changes. People will honor Jesus, the true altar, and offer prayers and praises according to his teachings.

Those who feel hurt or sad because God brought them low should find hope. God will heal their spirits and turn their cries into joyous songs. 

Gentiles and Jews will join together in following Jesus, the Good Shepherd. All these different groups will receive the same blessings from God. They will pray together at God’s throne and work together on religious matters. This unity should stop any arguments and bring believers closer in love.

21.o: Babylon was a flat country, abundantly watered. The destruction of Babylon, so often prophesied of by Isaiah, was typical of the destruction of the great foe of the New Testament church, foretold in the Revelation. To the poor oppressed captives it would be welcome news; to the proud oppressors it would be grievous. Let this check vain mirth and sensual pleasures, that we know not in what heaviness the mirth may end. Here is the alarm given to Babylon, when forced by Cyrus. An ass and a camel seem to be the symbols of the Medes and Persians. Babylon's idols shall be so far from protecting her, that they shall be broken down. True believers are the corn of God's floor; hypocrites are but as chaff and straw, with which the wheat is now mixed, but from which it shall be separated. The corn of God's floor must expect to be threshed by afflictions and persecutions. God's Israel of old was afflicted. Even then God owns it is his still. In all events concerning the church, past, present, and to come, we must look to God, who has power to do any thing for his church, and grace to do every thing that is for her good.
21.m:
Babylon was a flat land with plenty of water. The destruction of Babylon, talked about many times by Isaiah, shows us what will happen to the big enemy of the New Testament church, as told in the book of Revelation. For the poor people held captive, hearing this news would bring joy. But for those who were proud and mean, it would cause sorrow. This should remind us that our fun and pleasure could turn to sadness at any moment.

When Cyrus attacked Babylon, he gave an alarm. He used an ass and a camel to symbolize the Medes and Persians. Babylon’s idols won’t protect them; instead, they’ll be destroyed. 

True believers are like the valuable grain on God’s threshing floor. Hypocrites are just like the worthless chaff and straw mixed in with the grain. The true believers will be separated from the hypocrites through trials and troubles. 

God’s people have faced hardships before. Yet even during hard times, God claims them as his own. Whether looking back at history, living in the present, or thinking about the future, we need to remember that God has the power to help his church and always acts for its good.

21.o: God's prophets and ministers are as watchmen in the city in a time of peace, to see that all is safe. As watchmen in the camp in time of war, to warn of the motions of the enemy. After a long sleep in sin and security, it is time to rise, to awake out of sleep. We have a great deal of work to do, a long journey to go; it is time to be stirring. After a long dark night is there any hope of the day dawning? What tidings of the night? What happens to-night? We must never be secure. But many make curious inquiries of the watchmen. They would willingly have nice questions solved, or difficult prophecies interpreted; but they do not seek into the state of their own souls, about the way of salvation, and the path of duty. The watchman answers by way of prophecy. There comes first a morning of light, and peace, and opportunity; but afterward comes a night of trouble and calamity. If there be a morning of youth and health, there will come a night of sickness and old age; if a morning of prosperity in the family, in the public, yet we must look for changes. It is our wisdom to improve the present morning, in preparation for the night that is coming after it. Inquire, return, come. We are urged to do it quickly, for there is no time to trifle. Those that return and come to God, will find they have a great deal of work to do, and but little time to do it in.
21.m:
God’s prophets and ministers act like watchmen in a city during peaceful times, making sure everything is okay. They’re also like watchmen in a camp during war, warning everyone about the enemy’s movements.

After a long period of sleeping in sin and feeling too safe, it’s time to wake up. We have much work to do and a long journey ahead. Is there any hope that the darkness of night will soon end? What news from the night? What has happened tonight?

We should never feel too secure. Many people ask detailed questions of the watchmen. They want complex prophecies explained, but they don’t examine their own souls or think about the way to salvation and the right path to follow.

The watchman responds with a prophecy. First, there will be a morning of light, peace, and chances. Then, a night of trouble and hardship will come. If you have a morning of youth and good health, expect a night of illness and aging. If there is a morning of family or community success, still prepare for future changes.

It’s wise to use today well, preparing for what lies ahead. Ask questions now, turn back to God, and come to him. Hurry, because there isn't time to waste. When people return to God, they realize they have lots of work to do and very little time to do it.

21.o: The Arabians lived in tents, and kept cattle. A destroying army shall be brought upon them, and make them an easy prey. We know not what straits we may be brought into before we die. Those may know the want of necessary food who now eat bread to the full. Neither the skill of archers, nor the courage of mighty men, can protect from the judgments of God. That is poor glory, which will thus quickly come to nothing. Thus hath the Lord said to me; and no word of his shall fall to the ground. We may be sure the Strength of Israel will not lie. Happy are those only whose riches and glory are out of the reach of invaders; all other prosperity will speedily pass away.
21.m: The Arabians lived in tents and raised animals. An enemy army will attack them and take their possessions easily. We do not know what troubles we might face before we die. People who have plenty of food today could one day go hungry. Even skilled archers and brave warriors cannot protect against God’s judgment. Glory that disappears quickly is worthless. The Lord told me this, and none of His words will fail. We can trust that the power of Israel will always be true. Only those who have wealth and honor that cannot be taken by invaders are truly happy. All other success will soon end.

22.o: Why is Jerusalem in such terror? Her slain men are not slain with the sword, but with famine; or, slain with fear, disheartened. Their rulers fled, but were overtaken. The servants of God, who foresee and warn sinners of coming miseries, are affected by the prospect. But all the horrors of a city taken by storm, faintly shadow forth the terrors of the day of wrath.
22.m: Why is Jerusalem so scared? Her people did not die from fighting, but from hunger or fear. Their leaders ran away but got caught. People who know about future troubles feel sad about them. But even the worst things that happen when a city falls cannot compare to the fear on judgment day.

22.o: The weakness of Judah now appeared more than ever. Now also they discovered their carnal confidence and their carnal security. They looked to the fortifications. They made sure of water for the city. But they were regardless of God in all these preparations. They did not care for his glory in what they did. They did not depend upon him for a blessing on their endeavours. For every creature is to us what God makes it to be; and we must bless him for it, and use it for him. There was great contempt of God's wrath and justice, in contending with them. God's design was to humble them, and bring them to repentance. They walked contrary to this. Actual disbelief of another life after this, is at the bottom of the carnal security and brutish sensuality, which are the sin, the shame, and ruin of so great a part of mankind. God was displeased at this. It is a sin against the remedy, and it is not likely they should ever repent of it. Whether this unbelief works by presumption or despair, it produces the same contempt of God, and is a token that a man will perish wilfully.
22.m:
Judah showed its weakness more than ever before. They relied too much on physical defenses and safety measures. They focused on making sure the city had enough water. But they ignored God completely during these preparations. They didn't think about glorifying him or depending on him for success. 

Every thing is only useful if God makes it helpful to us. We need to thank God for everything and use it for his purposes. People showed great disrespect toward God’s anger and justice by fighting against him. 

God wanted to teach Judah humility and lead them to regret their actions. Instead, they acted opposite to God’s wishes. This worldliness and selfishness come from doubting life after death. Such thoughts cause many people to live without fear of God. 

This lack of faith upset God greatly. It shows they won’t ever truly turn back to him. Whether this doubt leads to overconfidence or hopelessness, it always results in ignoring God. And this means someone is choosing to face destruction.

22.o: This message to Shebna is a reproof of his pride, vanity, and security; what vanity is all earthly grandeur, which death will so soon end! What will it avail, whether we are laid in a magnificent tomb, or covered with the green sod? Those who, when in power, turn and toss others, will be justly turned and tossed themselves. Eliakim should be put into Shebna's place. Those called to places of trust and power, should seek to God for grace to enable them to do their duty. Eliakim's advancement is described. Our Lord Jesus describes his own power as Mediator, Rev. 3:7, that he has the key of David. His power in the kingdom of heaven, and in ordering all the affairs of that kingdom, is absolute. Rulers should be fathers to those under their government; and the honour men bring unto their families, by their piety and usefulness, is more to be valued than what they derive from them by their names and titles. The glory of this world gives a man no real worth or excellence; it is but hung upon him, and it will soon drop from him. Eliakim was compared to a nail in a sure place; all his family are said to depend upon him. In eastern houses, rows of large spikes were built up in the walls. Upon these the moveables and utensils were hung. Our Lord Jesus is as a nail in a sure place. That soul cannot perish, nor that concern fall to the ground, which is by faith hung upon Christ. He will set before the believer an open door, which no man can shut, and bring both body and soul to eternal glory. But those who neglect so great salvation will find, that when he shutteth none can open, whether it be shutting out from heaven, or shutting up in hell for ever.
22.m:
This message to Shebna criticizes his pride, vanity, and sense of safety. All earthly greatness ends quickly with death. It doesn't matter if we're buried in a fancy tomb or covered by grass. People who abuse others while in power will face similar treatment. 

Eliakim would take Shebna’s position. Leaders need God’s help to do their jobs well. Eliakim's rise to power is explained. Jesus talks about having control over keys, like opening doors, showing his authority. Rulers should care for those they lead. A person's honor comes from being good and helpful, not just from their name or title.

Worldly glory adds no true value. It's only attached to us and falls away fast. Eliakim was like a strong nail where things hang. In old homes, people hung items on nails in the wall. Jesus is like a solid nail too. Faith in Christ keeps souls safe and concerns secure. He opens paths to eternal life for believers. But those who ignore such salvation miss out. When Jesus closes a door, no one can reopen it, whether keeping someone out of heaven or trapping them in hell forever.

23.o: Tyre was the mart of the nations. She was noted for mirth and diversions; and this made her loth to consider the warnings God gave by his servants. Her merchants were princes, and lived like princes. Tyre being destroyed and laid waste, the merchants should abandon her. Flee to shift for thine own safety; but those that are uneasy in one place, will be so in another; for when God's judgments pursue sinners, they will overtake them. Whence shall all this trouble come? It is a destruction from the Almighty. God designed to convince men of the vanity and uncertainty of all earthly glory. Let the ruin of Tyre warn all places and persons to take heed of pride; for he who exalts himself shall be abased. God will do it, who has all power in his hand; but the Chaldeans shall be the instruments.
23.m:
Tyre was known for its markets where people bought and sold things. The city loved fun and entertainment, which kept them from listening to the warnings given by God’s messengers. 

The merchants in Tyre were rich and lived like kings. When Tyre was destroyed and left empty, the merchants would leave too. Run away to save yourself, but if you’re restless in one place, you’ll be restless everywhere. God’s punishments will catch up to sinners wherever they go.

Where did all this trouble start? It came from the almighty God. He wanted to show everyone that all earthly glory is vain and uncertain. The downfall of Tyre should remind us to beware of pride because whoever raises themselves up will be brought down. God, who holds all power, will do it through the hands of the Chaldeans.

23.o: The desolations of Tyre were not to be for ever. The Lord will visit Tyre in mercy. But when set at liberty, she will use her old arts of temptation. The love of worldly wealth is spiritual idolatry; and covetousness is spiritual idolatry. This directs those that have wealth, to use it in the service of God. When we abide with God in our worldly callings, when we do all in our power to further the gospel, then our merchandise and hire are holiness to the Lord, if we look to his glory. Christians should carry on business as God's servants, and use riches as his stewards.
23.m:
The troubles of Tyre would not last forever. The Lord would show mercy to Tyre someday. 

But once freed from trouble, Tyre would go back to using its old tricks to tempt people. Loving money too much is like worshiping an idol. Being greedy is also like worshiping an idol. 

This teaches us who have money to use it for God’s work. When we stay focused on God while doing our jobs, and help spread the good news, then what we earn becomes holy. It belongs to the Lord. 

Christians should run their businesses as God’s workers. They should handle their wealth as if they are managing it for him.

24.o: All whose treasures and happiness are laid up on earth, will soon be brought to want and misery. It is good to apply to ourselves what the Scripture says of the vanity and vexation of spirit which attend all things here below. Sin has turned the earth upside down; the earth is become quite different to man, from what it was when God first made it to be his habitation. It is, at the best, like a flower, which withers in the hands of those that please themselves with it, and lay it in their bosoms. The world we live in is a world of disappointment, a vale of tears; the children of men in it are but of few days, and full of trouble, See the power of God's curse, how it makes all empty, and lays waste all ranks and conditions. Sin brings these calamities upon the earth; it is polluted by the sins of men, therefore it is made desolate by God's judgments. Carnal joy will soon be at end, and the end of it is heaviness. God has many ways to imbitter wine and strong drink to those who love them; distemper of body, anguish of mind, and the ruin of the estate, will make strong drink bitter, and the delights of sense tasteless. Let men learn to mourn for sin, and rejoice in God; then no man, no event, can take their joy from them.
24.m:
People who treasure what they have on Earth will quickly find themselves poor and miserable. It’s wise to think about how pointless and frustrating life can be here on Earth. Sin has changed everything; the world isn’t the same place God created for humans to enjoy. Even beautiful flowers wilt away quickly, just like our pleasures fade. Life is often filled with sadness and short-lived joys. 

God’s punishment shows us how He turns everything empty and ruins lives at all levels. Sins make the world corrupt, so God sends judgment to destroy it. Worldly happiness doesn’t last long and ends in sorrow. God can turn even sweet drinks bitter through sickness, pain, or loss of wealth. People should grieve over their sins and find true joy in God instead. Then nothing or no one can steal their happiness.

24.o: There shall be a remnant preserved from the general ruin, and it shall be a devout and pious remnant. These few are dispersed; like the gleanings of the olive tree, hid under the leaves. The Lord knows those that are his; the world does not. When the mirth of carnal worldlings ceases, the joy of the saints is as lively as ever, because the covenant of grace, the fountain of their comforts, and the foundation of their hopes, never fails. Those who rejoice in the Lord can rejoice in tribulation, and by faith may triumph when all about them are in tears. They encourage their fellow-sufferers to do likewise, even those who are in the furnace of affliction. Or, in the valleys, low, dark, miry places. In every fire, even the hottest, in every place, even the remotest, let us keep up our good thoughts of God. If none of these trials move us, then we glorify the Lord in the fires.
24.m:
A small group will survive the great destruction. This group will be made up of godly people. 

These few believers are spread out everywhere, hidden among others, just like olives left on branches after harvesting.

The Lord knows His followers, even if the world doesn’t see them.

When worldly people stop being happy, the joy of the saints remains strong. Their joy comes from God’s promises, which never fail.

Those who find happiness in the Lord can still be joyful during hard times. By faith, they can celebrate even when everyone else is crying.

They help other believers stay strong too, even those suffering greatly.

Or, they support each other in difficult situations, whether in troubled times or far-off places.

In every trial, no matter where it happens, we should have good thoughts about God.

If nothing else makes us turn away, we honor the Lord through our troubles.

24.o: Believers may be driven into the uttermost parts of the earth; but they are singing, not sighing. Here is terror to sinners; the prophet laments the miseries he saw breaking in like a torrent; and the small number of believers. He foresees that sin would abound. The meaning is plain, that evil pursues sinners. Unsteady, uncertain are all these things. Worldly men think to dwell in the earth as in a palace, as in a castle; but it shall be removed like a cottage, like a lodge put up for the night. It shall fall and not rise again; but there shall be new heavens and a new earth, in which shall dwell nothing but righteousness. Sin is a burden to the whole creation; it is a heavy burden, under which it groans now, and will sink at last. The high ones, that are puffed up with their grandeur, that think themselves out of the reach of danger, God will visit for their pride and cruelty. Let us judge nothing before the time, though some shall be visited. None in this world should be secure, though their condition be ever so prosperous; nor need any despair, though their condition be ever so deplorable. God will be glorified in all this. But the mystery of Providence is not yet finished. The ruin of the Redeemer's enemies must make way for his kingdom, and then the Sun of Righteousness will appear in full glory. Happy are those who take warning by the sentence against others; every impenitent sinner will sink under his transgression, and rise no more, while believers enjoy everlasting bliss.
24.m:
Believers might end up far away in distant lands, but they keep singing instead of crying. This verse warns sinners about trouble coming their way. The prophet talks about the many troubles he sees happening quickly, like a flood. There aren’t many people following God’s path. 

The writer predicts that bad behavior will increase. Evil follows those who do wrong. Everything changes without warning. People focus on living comfortably here on Earth, thinking they’re safe in big houses. But one day, even those homes will disappear, just like tents set up overnight. They’ll collapse and won’t stand again.

But after that, there will be new skies and new land where only good things happen. Sin causes problems everywhere, making nature suffer now and causing destruction later. Those who feel powerful because of their wealth and status will face God’s judgment for being proud and cruel. We shouldn’t rush to judge anyone, since some people will indeed face consequences.

No one should feel too sure about their future, whether life seems great or terrible right now. God will show His greatness through everything that happens. However, we don’t fully understand all of God’s plans yet. The downfall of those who oppose Jesus will lead to His victory. Then, the light of goodness will shine brightly.

Those who learn from seeing what happens to others will find happiness. Unrepentant sinners will face their mistakes and won’t get back up. Meanwhile, faithful followers will have eternal joy.

25.o: However this might show the deliverance of the Jews out of captivity, it looked further, to the praises that should be offered up to God for Christ's victories over our spiritual enemies, and the comforts he has provided for all believers. True faith simply credits the Lord's testimony, and relies on his truth to perform his promises. As God weakens the strong who are proud and secure, so he strengthens the weak that are humble, and stay themselves upon him. God protects his people in all weathers. The Lord shelters those who trust in him from the insolence of oppressors. Their insolence is but the noise of strangers; it is like the heat of the sun scorching in the middle of the day; but where is it when the sun is set? The Lord ever was, and ever will be, the Refuge of distressed believers. Having provided them a shelter, he teaches them to flee unto it.
25.m:
This could show how God freed the Jews from their captors. It also points to the praise that should be given to God for Jesus' victories over our spiritual enemies and the comfort he gives to all believers. 

True faith believes what God says and trusts that he will keep his promises. When God weakens the proud and confident, he strengthens those who are humble and rely on him. God takes care of his people through all kinds of weather. He shields those who put their trust in him from mean-spirited bullies. 

The bullying is just like the hot sun at midday—it goes away once the sun sets. The Lord always has been and always will be a safe place for troubled believers. After providing them with a shelter, he guides them to run to it.

25.o: The kind reception of repentant sinners, is often in the New Testament likened to a feast. The guests invited are all people, Gentiles as well as Jews. There is that in the gospel which strengthens and makes glad the heart, and is fit for those who are under convictions of sin, and mourning for it. There is a veil spread over all nations, for all sat in darkness. But this veil the Lord will destroy, by the light of his gospel shining in the world, and the power of his Spirit opening men's eyes to receive it. He will raise those to spiritual life who were long dead in trespasses and sins. Christ will himself, in his resurrection, triumph over death. Grief shall be banished; there shall be perfect and endless joy. Those that mourn for sin shall be comforted. Those who suffer for Christ shall have consolations. But in the joys of heaven, and not short of them, will fully be brought to pass this saying, God shall wipe away all tears. The hope of this should now do away over-sorrow, all weeping that hinders sowing. Sometimes, in this world God takes away the reproach of his people from among men; however, it will be done fully at the great day. Let us patiently bear sorrow and shame now; both will be done away shortly.
25.m:
The welcoming of people who turn from their sins is often compared to a feast in the New Testament. Both Jews and Gentiles are invited to join. 

There is joy and strength in the message of the gospel, which helps those feeling guilty about their sins. A veil covers everyone, hiding them in darkness because of their wrongdoings. But Jesus removes this veil through the light of his teachings and the work of his Spirit, helping people see clearly.

Jesus brings new life to those who were once spiritually dead because of their sins. His resurrection shows victory over death. Sorrow will end, replaced by perfect and lasting happiness. People who feel sad about their sins will find comfort. Christians who face suffering will receive peace.

In heaven, all sadness will go away completely. This promise should help us not be too upset here on earth, since our tears won't last forever. Sometimes, even now, God lifts the shame from his followers. At the end of time, he will do so fully. We should endure hardship and disgrace patiently, knowing they will soon disappear.

25.o: With joy and praise will those entertain the glad tidings of the Redeemer, who looked for him; and with a triumphant song will glorified saints enter into the joy of their Lord. And it is not in vain to wait for him; for the mercy comes at last, with abundant recompence for the delay. The hands once stretched out upon the cross, to make way for our salvation, will at length be stretched forth to destroy all impenitent sinners. Moab is here put for all adversaries of God's people; they shall all be trodden down or threshed. God shall bring down the pride of the enemies by one humbling judgment after another. This destruction of Moab is typical of Christ's victory, and the pulling down of Satan's strong holds. Therefore, beloved brethren, be ye stedfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord; for your labour is not in vain in the Lord.
25.m:
Those who look forward to the Redeemer will welcome his good news with joy and praise. Glorified saints will sing triumphantly as they enter into their Lord’s joy. It is not pointless to wait for him because his mercy will come eventually, bringing plenty of rewards for waiting.

The hands that were once stretched out on the cross to save us will finally be used to punish all those who do not repent. Here, Moab represents all enemies of God’s people, and they will all be crushed or beaten down. God will humble the pride of these enemies through many judgments. 

This defeat of Moab shows what will happen when Christ wins and pulls down Satan’s strongholds. So dear brothers and sisters, stay firm and keep working hard for the Lord. Your efforts are never wasted in serving him.

26.o: "That day," seems to mean when the New Testament Babylon shall be levelled with the ground. The unchangeable promise and covenant of the Lord are the walls of the church of God. The gates of this city shall be open. Let sinners then be encouraged to join to the Lord. Thou wilt keep him in peace; in perfect peace, inward peace, outward peace, peace with God, peace of conscience, peace at all times, in all events. Trust in the Lord for that peace, that portion, which will be for ever. Whatever we trust to the world for, it will last only for a moment; but those who trust in God shall not only find in him, but shall receive from him, strength that will carry them to that blessedness which is for ever. Let us then acknowledge him in all our ways, and rely on him in all trials.
26.m: That day likely means when the New Testament Babylon will be destroyed. The promises and covenants of the Lord are what protect his church. The doors of this city will always be open. Sinners should feel free to join the Lord. He will keep you safe and give you inner peace, outer peace, peace with God, peace of mind, and peace at all times. Put your trust in the Lord for that kind of lasting peace. Anything you seek from the world won’t last long, but those who trust in God will receive strength that lasts forever. So let’s recognize God in everything we do and depend on him during hard times.

26.o: The way of the just is evenness, a steady course of obedience and holy conversation. And it is their happiness that God makes their way plain and easy. It is our duty, and will be our comfort, to wait for God, to keep up holy desires toward him in the darkest and most discouraging times. Our troubles must never turn us from God; and in the darkest, longest night of affliction, with our souls must we desire him; and this we must wait and pray to him for. We make nothing of our religion, whatever our profession may be, if we do not make heart-work of it. Though we come ever so early, we shall find God ready to receive us. The intention of afflictions is to teach righteousness: blessed is the man whom the Lord thus teaches. But sinners walk contrary to him. They will go on in their evil ways, because they will not consider what a God he is whose laws they persist in despising. Scorners and the secure will shortly feel, what now they will not believe, that it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God. They will not see the evil of sin; but they shall see. Oh that they would abandon their sins, and turn to the Lord, that he may have mercy upon them.
26.m:
The path of the righteous is smooth and steady, following God’s commands and living a good life. Their joy comes from God making their journey clear and easy. 

It’s our responsibility—and comfort—to trust God during hard times. Even in the darkest moments, we should long for him. Our struggles shouldn’t push us away from God. In the toughest times, we must still seek him with our hearts.

If we truly follow our faith, not just pretend, we’ll find God waiting for us whenever we reach out. 

God sends trials to teach us right from wrong. Those who learn from these lessons are blessed. But sinners ignore God’s teachings. 

They continue doing bad things without thinking about who God really is. Mockers and those who think they’re safe won’t realize until it’s too late how scary it is to face God’s judgment. 

Now they don’t see the harm in their sins, but soon enough they will. I hope they stop sinning and turn back to God before it’s too late.

26.o: Every creature, every business, any way serviceable to our comfort, God makes to be so; he makes that work for us which seemed to make against us. They had been slaves of sin and Satan; but by the Divine grace they were taught to look to be set free from all former masters. The cause opposed to God and his kingdom will sink at last. See our need of afflictions. Before, prayer came drop by drop; now they pour it out, it comes now like water from a fountain. Afflictions bring us to secret prayer. Consider Christ as the Speaker addressing his church. His resurrection from the dead was an earnest of all the deliverance foretold. The power of his grace, like the dew or rain, which causes the herbs that seem dead to revive, would raise his church from the lowest state. But we may refer to the resurrection of the dead, especially of those united to Christ.
26.m:
Every creature and task that helps our comfort, God uses to do just that. He turns what seems to go against us into something that works for us. They used to be slaves to sin and Satan, but through God’s grace, they learned to expect freedom from their old masters. 

The forces that oppose God and his kingdom will eventually fail. We see how much we need troubles to grow. Before, their prayers were few, but now they pray freely, like water flowing from a fountain. Hardships lead people to pray more deeply. Think of Jesus speaking to his church. His rising from death showed that all the promised rescues would happen. Just as dew or rain revives plants that look dead, God’s grace brings his church back to life from its lowest points. This could also mean the resurrection of the dead, especially those connected to Christ.

26.o: When dangers threaten, it is good to retire and lie hid; when we commend ourselves to God to hide us, he will hide us either under heaven or in heaven. Thus we shall be safe and happy in the midst of tribulations. It is but for a short time, as it were for a little moment; when over, it will seem as nothing. God's place is the mercy-seat; there he delights to be: when he punishes, he comes out of his place, for he has no pleasure in the death of sinners. But there is hardly any truth more frequently repeated in Scripture, than God's determined purpose to punish the workers of iniquity. Let us keep close to the Lord, and separate from the world; and let us seek comfort in secret prayer. A day of vengeance is coming on the world, and before it comes we are to expect tribulation and suffering. But because the Christian looks for these things, shall he be restless and dismayed? No, let him repose himself in his God. Abiding in him, the believer is safe. And let us wait patiently the fulfilling of God's promises.
26.m:
When danger comes, it’s best to go somewhere safe and hide. When we ask God to protect us, he might hide us under his care here on earth or even take us to heaven. This way, we’ll stay safe and happy even during hard times. 

These troubles won’t last long—they feel big now, but soon they’ll be over. God usually stays at his mercy seat, where he loves to be. He only leaves it to punish people who do wrong, which doesn't bring him joy. 

The Bible often talks about God punishing those who do evil deeds. We should stay close to God and away from the world. We need to find peace through private prayers. 

A day of judgment is coming for everyone, so we can expect some trouble before then. But Christians shouldn’t worry if they know these tough times are coming. They can rest in God’s protection. 

By staying connected to God, believers remain safe. And we should calmly wait for God’s promises to come true.

27.o: The Lord Jesus with his strong sword, the virtue of his death, and the preaching of his gospel, does and will destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil, that old serpent. The world is a fruitless, worthless wilderness; but the church is a vineyard, a place that has great care taken of it, and from which precious fruits are gathered. God will keep it in the night of affliction and persecution, and in the day of peace and prosperity, the temptations of which are not less dangerous. God also takes care of the fruitfulness of this vineyard. We need the continual waterings of Divine grace; if these be at any time withdrawn, we wither, and come to nothing. Though God sometimes contends with his people, yet he graciously waits to be reconciled unto them. It is true, when he finds briers and thorns instead of vines, and they are set in array against him, he will tread them down and burn them. Here is a summary of the doctrine of the gospel, with which the church is to be watered every moment. Ever since sin first entered, there has been, on God's part, a righteous quarrel, but, on man's part, most unrighteous. Here is a gracious invitation given. Pardoning mercy is called the power of our Lord; let us take hold on that. Christ crucified is the power of God. Let us by lively faith take hold on his strength who is a strength to the needy, believing there is no other name by which we can be saved, as a man that is sinking catches hold of a bough, or cord, or plank, that is in his reach. This is the only way, and it is a sure way, to be saved. God is willing to be reconciled to us.
27.m:
Jesus uses his powerful sword, which is the value of his death and the message of his gospel, to defeat the devil, the one who holds the power of death. 

The world is like an empty, useless desert, but the church is like a well-cared-for vineyard where valuable things grow. God protects the church during times of trouble and persecution, as well as during peaceful and prosperous times, because both have their dangers. 

God also looks after the growth of the church. We need God’s grace to keep growing, and without it, we dry up and die. Even though God may discipline his people, he always wants to forgive them. 

Sometimes, when people act wrongly toward God, he punishes them. But God still offers forgiveness. The gospel teaches us about God’s love and mercy, which we should believe in all the time. 

Since sin came into the world, God has been justly angry, while humans have acted unfairly. Yet God invites everyone to receive his pardon. 

Pardon from God is called the power of our Lord. We should grasp onto that. Christ’s death on the cross gives us strength. By having faith, we can trust in him, knowing he is the only one who can save us. Like someone drowning who grabs anything within reach, we should cling to Christ for salvation. 

This is the only path to safety, and it works. God is ready to forgive us and reconcile with us.

27.o: In the days of the gospel, the latter days, the gospel church shall be more firmly fixed than the Jewish church, and shall spread further. May our souls be continually watered and kept, that we may abound in the fruits of the Spirit, in all goodness, righteousness, and truth. The Jews yet are kept a separate and a numerous people; they have not been rooted out as those who slew them. The condition of that nation, through so many ages, forms a certain proof of the Divine origin of the Scriptures; and the Jews live amongst us, a continued warning against sin. But though winds are ever so rough, ever so high, God can say to them, Peace, be still. And though God will afflict his people, yet he will make their afflictions to work for the good of their souls. According to this promise, since the captivity in Babylon, no people have shown such hatred to idols and idolatry as the Jews. And to all God's people, the design of affliction is to part between them and sin. The affliction has done us good, when we keep at a distance from the occasions of sin, and use care that we may not be tempted to it. Jerusalem had been defended by grace and the Divine protection; but when God withdrew, she was left like a wilderness. This has awfully come to pass. And this is a figure of the deplorable state of the vineyard, the church, when it brought forth wild grapes. Sinners flatter themselves they shall not be dealt with severely, because God is merciful, and is their Maker. We see how weak those pleas will be. Verses 12,13, seem to predict the restoration of the Jews after the Babylonish captivity, and their recovery from their present dispersion. This is further applicable to the preaching of the gospel, by which sinners are gathered into the grace of God; the gospel proclaims the acceptable year of the Lord. Those gathered by the sounding of the gospel trumpet, are brought in to worship God, and added to the church; and the last trumpet will gather the saints together.
27.m:
In the time of the gospel, the Christian church will be stronger and grow bigger than the Jewish one. Let’s ask God to help us produce lots of good things—kindness, honesty, and truth.

The Jewish people remain separate and large even today. They were not wiped out, despite killing Jesus. Their long history proves the Bible came from God. They serve as a constant reminder to avoid sin.

Even during storms, God can command peace. He might test His followers, but these trials aim to improve their spirits. Since returning from Babylon, no group has hated false gods as much as the Jews do now. Affliction helps believers stay away from sin.

God protected Jerusalem until He stopped. Then it became deserted. This shows what happens when the church produces bad fruit. Some think God won’t punish them because He’s kind and made them. But we see how useless those excuses are.

Verses 12 and 13 talk about the Jews coming back after being held captive in Babylon. It also applies to people joining God’s family through the gospel. When the gospel sounds, sinners turn to God and join the church. The final trumpet call will bring all believers together.

28.o: What men are proud of, be it ever so mean, is to them as a crown; but pride is the forerunner of destruction. How foolishly drunkards act! Those who are overcome with wine are overcome by Satan; and there is not greater drudgery in the world than hard drinking. Their health is ruined; men are broken in their callings and estates, and their families are ruined by it. Their souls are in danger of being undone for ever, and all merely to gratify a base lust. In God's professing people, like Israel, it is worse than in any other. And he is just in taking away the plenty they thus abuse. The plenty they were proud of, is but a fading flower. Like the early fruit, which, as soon as discovered, is plucked and eaten.
28.m: What people are proud of, even if it’s not important, feels like a crown to them. But pride leads to ruin. Drunkards act very foolishly. People who get drunk let themselves be controlled by Satan. There is no harder work than heavy drinking. It ruins their health, breaks down their jobs and wealth, and wrecks their families. They risk losing their souls forever just to satisfy a low desire. For those who claim to follow God, like the Israelites, it’s even worse. God is right to take away the abundance they misuse. The things they boast about are like flowers that quickly fade. Just like early fruit, once found, is picked and eaten.

28.o: The prophet next turns to Judah, whom he calls the residue of his people. Happy are those alone, who glory in the Lord of hosts himself. Hence his people get wisdom and strength for every service and every conflict. But it is only in Christ Jesus that the holy God communicates with sinful man. And whether those that teach are drunk with wine, or intoxicated with false doctrines and notions concerning the kingdom and salvation of the Messiah, they not only err themselves, but lead multitudes astray. All places where such persons have taught are filled with errors. For our instruction in the things of God, it is needful that the same precept and the same line should be often repeated to us, that we may the better understand them. God, by his word, calls us to what is really for our advantage; the service of God is the only true rest for those weary of the service of sin, and there is no refreshment but under the easy yoke of the Lord Jesus. All this had little effect upon the people. Those who will not understand what is plain, but scorn and despise it as mean and trifling, are justly punished. If we are at peace with God, we have, in effect, made a covenant with death; whenever it comes, it cannot do us any real damage, if we are Christ's. But to think of making death our friend, while by sin we are making God our enemy, is absurd. And do not they make lies their refuge who trust in their own righteousness, or to a death-bed repentance? which is a resolution to sin no more, when it is no longer in their power to do so.
28.m:
The prophet then talks about Judah, calling them the remaining part of his people. Only those who take pride in the Lord of armies are truly happy. So, his people gain wisdom and strength for all tasks and battles. But it’s only through Christ Jesus that the holy God connects with sinful humans. 

Whether teachers are drunk from wine or misled by wrong ideas about the kingdom and salvation of the Messiah, they mislead others too. Places where these teachers work are full of mistakes. To help us learn about God, the same teachings are repeated many times so we can understand better. 

God uses his word to guide us toward what’s best for us. Serving God is the only true rest for those tired of serving sin. The easiest way to find peace is under the gentle rule of Jesus. This message didn’t affect the people much. People who refuse to listen to clear truths because they think it’s unimportant deserve punishment. 

If we are at peace with God, we’ve made an agreement with death. When it comes, it won’t harm us if we belong to Christ. But trying to make death friendly while angering God through sin doesn’t make sense. Don’t those who hide behind lies trust in their own goodness or last-minute regrets? That means deciding to stop sinning right before dying.

28.o: Here is a promise of Christ, as the only foundation of hope for escaping the wrath to come. This foundation was laid in Zion, in the eternal counsels of God. This foundation is a stone, firm and able to support his church. It is a tried stone, a chosen stone, approved of God, and never failed any who made trial of it. A corner stone, binding together the whole building, and bearing the whole weight; precious in the sight of the Lord, and of every believer; a sure foundation on which to build. And he who in any age or nation shall believe this testimony, and rest all his hopes, and his never-dying soul on this foundation, shall never be confounded. The right effect of faith in Christ is, to quiet and calm the soul, till events shall be timed by Him, who has all times in his own hand and power. Whatever men trust to for justification, except the righteousness of Christ; or for wisdom, strength, and holiness, except the influences of the Holy Ghost; or for happiness, except the favour of God; that protection in which they thought to shelter themselves, will prove not enough to answer the intention. Those who rest in a righteousness of their own, will have deceived themselves: the bed is too short, the covering too narrow. God will be glorified in the fulfilling of his counsels. If those that profess to be members of God's church, make themselves like Philistines and Canaanites, they must expect to be dealt with as such. Then dare not to ridicule the reproofs of God's word, or the approaches of judgements.
28.m:
Here is a promise about Christ being the only way to escape God’s punishment. This promise was part of God’s plans from the beginning. 

Christ is called a stone, strong and dependable. He is a tested stone, one that God chose because it works well. Anyone who tries him finds he doesn’t fail. He is also a cornerstone, holding everything together and supporting the whole structure. To believers, he is very valuable. Building on him gives us a solid base.

Anyone at any time who believes in this truth and puts all their hopes in Christ will never regret it. Faith in Christ calms our souls until God decides what happens next. We know God controls all timing.

If people rely on anything else—like their own goodness—for forgiveness, wisdom, strength, or happiness, they will be disappointed. Their efforts won't protect them. People who depend on their own righteousness will fool themselves. They’ll find their efforts aren’t enough.

God will show his glory by making his promises true. When church members act like outsiders, they should expect to face consequences. Don’t laugh at God’s warnings or ignore signs of trouble coming.

28.o: The husbandman applies to his calling with pains and prudence, in all the works of it according to their nature. Thus the Lord, who has given men this wisdom, is wonderful in counsel, and excellent in his working. As the occasion requires, he threatens, corrects, spares, shows mercy, or executes vengeance. Afflictions are God's threshing instruments, to loosen us from the world, to part between us and our chaff, and to prepare us for use. God will proportion them to our strength; they shall be no heavier than there is need. When his end is answered, the trials and sufferings of his people shall cease; his wheat shall be gathered into the garner, but the chaff shall be burned with unquenchable fire.
28.m: The farmer works hard and wisely at his job, doing each task carefully. This shows how God gives people wisdom and is amazing in planning and working. Depending on what’s needed, God may warn, punish, show kindness, or take revenge. Troubles are like God’s tools to separate us from the world and its bad parts. He makes sure these troubles fit our strength, not too much. Once His purpose is done, the suffering stops. The good people go to heaven, while those who don’t listen are punished forever.

29.o: Ariel may signify the altar of burnt-offerings. Let Jerusalem know that outward religious services will not make men free from judgements. Hypocrites never can please God, nor make their peace with him. God had often and long, by a host of angels, encamped round about Jerusalem for protection and deliverance; but now he fought against it. Proud looks and proud language shall be brought down by humbling providences. The destruction of Jerusalem's enemies is foretold. The army of Sennacherib went as a dream; and thus the multitudes, that through successive ages fight against God's altar and worship, shall fall. Speedily will sinners awake from their soothing dreams in the pains of hell.
29.m:
Ariel might mean the altar of burnt offerings. Let Jerusalem know that just doing religious things won’t keep people safe from trouble. Hypocrites can't please God or make peace with him. 

God has often sent many angels to protect Jerusalem. But now he fights against it. Proud attitudes and words will be brought low by humble times. 

The destruction of Jerusalem’s enemies is predicted. When Sennacherib’s army came, it was like a bad dream. And all those who fight against God’s altar and worship over time will fall too. Sinners will wake up from their peaceful dreams to face the pain of hell soon.

29.o: The security of sinners in sinful ways, is cause for lamentation and wonder. The learned men, through prejudice, said that the Divine prophecies were obscure; and the poor urged their want of learning. The Bible is a sealed book to every man, learned or unlearned, till he begins to study it with a simple heart and a teachable spirit, that he may thence learn the truth and the will of God. To worship God, is to approach him. And if the heart be full of his love and fear, out of the abundance of it the mouth will speak; but there are many whose religion is lip-labour only. When they pretend to be speaking to God, they are thinking of a thousand foolish things. They worship the God of Israel according to their own devices. Numbers are only formal in worship. And their religion is only to comply with custom, and to serve their own interest. But the wanderings of mind, and defects in devotion, which are the believer's burden, are very different from the withdrawing of the heart from God, so severely blamed. And those who make religion no more than a pretence, to serve a turn, deceive themselves. And as those that quarrel with God, so those that think to conceal themselves from him, in effect charge him with folly. But all their perverse conduct shall be entirely done away.
29.m:
The safety of people who do wrong things is sad and surprising. Learned people thought the prophecies were hard to understand because of their biases. Poor people said they lacked education. 

For everyone, whether smart or not, the Bible stays locked until they start studying it with an open and teachable mind. Worshiping God means coming close to Him. If someone’s heart is filled with His love and respect, they’ll talk about it freely. But many people only use words without feeling.

When they claim to pray, their minds wander to silly thoughts instead. They worship God based on their own ideas. Many just go through the motions during worship. Their faith serves customs and personal interests.

A believer’s struggle with wandering thoughts and weak prayers is different from completely turning away from God, which is strongly criticized. People who pretend to have real faith trick themselves. Those who argue with God or try to hide from Him show He doesn’t know what’s happening.

But all their bad behavior will eventually end.

29.o: The wonderful change here foretold, may refer to the affairs of Judah, though it looks further. When a great harvest of souls was gathered to Christ from among the Gentiles, then the wilderness was turned into a fruitful field; and the Jewish church, that had long been a fruitful field, became as a deserted forest. Those who, when in trouble, can truly rejoice in God, shall soon have cause greatly to rejoice in him. The grace of meekness contributes to the increase of our holy joy. The enemies who were powerful shall become mean and weak. To complete the repose of God's people, the scorners at home shall be cut off by judgements. All are apt to speak unadvisedly, and to mistake what they hear, but it is very unfair to make a man an offender for a word. They did all they could to bring those into trouble who told them of their faults. But He that redeemed Abraham out of his snares and troubles, will redeem those who are, by faith, his true seed, out of theirs. It will be the greatest comfort to godly parents to see their children renewed creatures, the work of God's grace. May those who now err in spirit, and murmur against the truth, come to understanding, and learn true doctrine. The Spirit of truth shall set right their mistakes, and lead them into all truth. This should encourage us to pray for those that have erred, and are deceived. All who murmured at the truths of God, as hard sayings, shall learn and be aware what God designed in all. See the change religion produces in the hearts of men, and the peace and pleasure of a humble and devout spirit.
29.m:
The amazing change described here might refer to events involving Judah, even if it looks ahead to more distant times. When many new followers joined Christ from among the Gentiles, the desert areas became rich farmland. And the once-productive Jewish church became like a deserted forest. People who can truly rejoice in God during tough times will soon find much reason to celebrate. Being gentle helps grow our joy.

Powerful enemies will become weak and powerless. For real peace, God’s people need to deal with the mockers at home through judgment. Everyone talks without thinking sometimes, and often misunderstands things, but it’s wrong to punish someone just because of words. They tried to get people in trouble for pointing out their faults. But God who saved Abraham from problems will save those who believe in Him today.

It brings great joy to godly parents to see their children changed by God’s grace. Let those who now doubt and complain about the truth gain understanding and learn correct teachings. The Holy Spirit will fix their mistakes and guide them toward all truth. This should inspire us to pray for those who are confused and misled. Anyone who complained about God’s teachings as too difficult will eventually understand His intentions.

See how religion changes people’s hearts, bringing peace and happiness to those who humbly follow God.

30.o: It was often the fault and folly of the Jews, that when troubled by their neighbours on one side, they sought for succour from others, instead of looking up to God. Nor can we avoid the dreadful consequences of adding sin to sin, but by making the righteousness of Christ our refuge, and seeking for the sanctification of the Holy Spirit. Men have always been prone to lean to their own understandings, but this will end in their shame and misery. They would not trust in God. They took much pains to gain the Egyptians. The riches so spent turned to a bad account. See what dangers men run into who forsake God to follow their carnal confidences. The Creator is the Rock of ages, the creature a broken reed; we cannot expect too little from man, or too much from God. Our strength is to sit still, in humble dependence upon God and his goodness, and quiet submission to his will.
30.m:
The Jews often made mistakes when they were troubled by enemies. Instead of turning to God for help, they looked to other people. We can only escape the terrible results of piling more sins on top of our old ones if we rely on Jesus' righteousness and ask the Holy Spirit to make us holy. 

People tend to depend on their own ideas, which leads to shame and trouble. The Israelites did not trust God. They tried hard to get support from Egypt. But spending all that money on them didn't work out well. When people leave God to follow worldly hopes, they face many dangers. 

God is our solid rock, while humans are weak supports that break easily. We should expect very little from people and everything from God. Our best path is to stay calm and trust in God’s kindness and will.

30.o: The Jews were the only professing people God then had in the world, yet many among them were rebellious. They had the light, but they loved darkness rather. The prophets checked them in their sinful pursuits, so that they could not proceed without fear; this they took amiss. But faithful ministers will not be driven from seeking to awaken sinners. God is the Holy One of Israel, and so they shall find him. They did not like to hear of his holy commandments and his hatred of sin; they desired that they might no more be reminded of these things. But as they despised the word of God, their sins undermined their safety. Their state would be dashed in pieces like a potter's vessel. Let us return from our evil ways, and settle in the way of duty; that is the way to be saved. Would we be strengthened, it must be in quietness and in confidence, keeping peace in our own minds, and relying upon God. They think themselves wiser than God; but the project by which they thought to save themselves was their ruin. Only here and there one shall escape, as a warning to others. If men will not repent, turn to God, and seek happiness in his favour and service, their desires will but hasten their ruin. Those who make God alone their confidence, will have comfort. God ever waits to be gracious to all that come to him by faith in Christ, and happy are those who wait for him.
30.m:
The Jews were the only group of people worshipping God at that time, but many of them were rebellious. Even though they had the truth, they preferred to stay in the dark. Prophets tried to stop them from doing wrong, making them feel afraid if they continued. But the prophets wouldn’t give up trying to wake up sinners. 

God is the Holy One of Israel, and he remains that way. People didn't want to hear about his rules or his dislike of sin. They wished they wouldn't be reminded of such things anymore. Yet, because they ignored God’s words, their actions led to their downfall. 

Their situation would end badly, just like breaking a clay pot. We should leave our bad habits and start following what’s right. That’s the path to being saved. To become stronger, we need to be peaceful and trust in God. 

Some people think they know better than God. Their plans to save themselves actually brought destruction instead. Only a few will survive as a lesson to others. If people don’t change their ways and turn to God, their wishes will lead to their ruin. Those who put their full trust in God receive comfort. God always waits to bless anyone who comes to him through faith in Jesus. And those who wait on God are truly blessed.

30.o: God's people will soon arrive at the Zion above, and then they will weep no more for ever. Even now they would have more comfort, as well as holiness, if they were more constant in prayer. A famine of bread is not so great a judgment as a famine of the word of God. There are right-hand and left-hand errors; the tempter is busy courting us into by-paths. It is happy if, by the counsels of a faithful minister or friend, or the checks of conscience, and the strivings of God the Spirit, we are set right when doubting, and prevented from going wrong. They shall be cured of their idolatry. To all true penitents sin becomes very hateful. This is shown daily in the conversion of souls, by the power of Divine grace, to the fear and love of God. Abundant means of grace, with the influences of the Holy Spirit, would be extended to places destitute of them. The effect of this should be comfort and joy to the people of God. Light, that is, knowledge, shall increase. This is the light which the gospel brought into the world, and which proclaims healing to the broken-hearted.
30.m:
God’s people will soon reach heaven, where they won’t cry anymore forever. Even now, they could find more happiness and stay holy if they prayed more often. Hunger for food isn't as bad as hunger for God’s word. 

There are mistakes on both sides, and the devil tries to lead us off course. But it’s good when a trusted pastor, friend, or our conscience helps us get back on track when we doubt. People will stop worshiping false gods. For those who truly feel sorry, sin becomes very hateful. We see this happen every day as people turn to God through His grace.

The Holy Spirit and godly friends help bring comfort and joy to believers. Places without access to God’s word will receive it. Knowledge will grow. This is the truth that the gospel brings, bringing hope to those who are sad.

30.o: God curbs and restrains from doing mischief. With a word he guides his people into the right way, but with a bridle he turns his enemies upon their own ruin. Here, in threatening the ruin of Sennacherib's army, the prophet points at the final and everlasting destruction of all impenitent sinners. Tophet was a valley near Jerusalem, where fires were continually burning to destroy things that were hurtful and offensive, and there the idolatrous Jews caused their children to pass through the fire to Moloch. This denotes the certainty of the destruction, as an awful emblem of the place of torment in the other world. No oppressor shall escape the Divine wrath. Let sinners then flee to Christ, seeking to be reconciled to Him, that they may be safe and happy, when destruction from the Almighty shall sweep away all the workers of iniquity.
30.m:
God stops people from doing bad things. He guides his followers with gentle words, but he controls those who oppose him by leading them to their own downfall. When the prophet warned about the defeat of Sennacherib’s army, he was also talking about the eternal punishment for all unrepentant sinners. 

Tophet was a valley close to Jerusalem where constant fires burned to get rid of harmful things. The sinful Jews used to burn their children there as sacrifices to the god Moloch. This shows how surely God will punish sin, just like the fires destroyed the offerings. 

No one who hurts others will avoid God’s anger. So, sinners should turn to Christ and ask for forgiveness so they can find safety and happiness instead of facing the terrible judgment that comes from God.

31.o: God will oppose the help sought from workers of iniquity. Sinners may be convicted of folly by plain and self-evident truths, which they cannot deny, but will not believe. There is no escaping the judgments of God; and evil pursues sinners. The Lord of hosts will come down to fight for Mount Zion. The Lion of the tribe of Judah will appear for the defence of his church. And as birds hovering over their young ones to protect them, with such compassion and affection will the Lord of hosts defend Jerusalem. He will so defend it, as to secure its safety.
31.m:
God won't support people who ask help from those who do wrong things. Sinners might see obvious truths that they can’t deny, but still won’t believe them. People can’t escape God’s judgment, and bad choices bring trouble. 

The Lord of armies will fight for Mount Zion. The Lion from the tribe of Judah will show up to protect his church. Just like birds hover over their babies to keep them safe, God will watch over Jerusalem with love and care. He will guard it to keep it safe.

31.o: They have been backsliding children, yet children; let them return, and their backslidings shall be healed, though they have sunk deep into misery, and cannot easily recover. Many make an idol of their silver and gold, and by the love of that are drawn from God; but those who turn to God, will be ready to part with it. Then, when they have cast away their idols, shall the Assyrian fall by the sword of an angel, who strikes more strongly than a mighty man, yet more secretly than a mean man. God can make the stoutest heart to tremble. But if we keep up the fire of holy love and devotion in our hearts and houses, we may depend upon God to protect us and them.
31.m:
They have been straying children, but still children. Let them come back, and their mistakes will be fixed, even if they’ve fallen into great trouble and find it hard to get out. 

Many people worship money instead of God, letting greed pull them away. But those who turn to God will gladly give up their wealth. When they abandon their false gods, the Assyrians will fall because of an angel’s strike, which hits harder than a strong warrior but quieter than a weak one. 

God can make the bravest heart shake with fear. Yet, if we keep the flame of holy love burning in our hearts and homes, we can trust God to guard us and them.

32.o: Christ our righteous King, and his true disciples, are evidently here intended. The consolations and graces of his Spirit are as rivers of water in this dry land; and as the overhanging rock affords refreshing shade and shelter to the weary traveller in the desert, so his power, truth, and love, yield the believer the only real protection and refreshment in the weary land through which he journeys to heaven. Christ bore the storm himself, to keep it off from us. To him let the trembling sinner flee for refuge; for he alone can protect and refresh us in every trial. See what pains sinners take in sin; they labour at it, their hearts are intent upon it, and with art they work iniquity; but this is our comfort, that they can do no more mischief than God permits. Let us seek to have our hearts more freed from selfishness. The liberal soul devises liberal things concerning God, and desires that He will grant wisdom and prudence, the comforts of his presence, the influence of his Spirit, and in due time the enjoyment of his glory.
32.m:
Christ is our righteous king, and his true followers are meant here. His Spirit gives us comfort and grace, just like rivers give water to dry land. And just like a big rock provides cool shade for someone tired and hot in the desert, Christ’s power, truth, and love offer us the only real safety and relief on our journey to heaven. 

Christ faced the storms himself to keep them away from us. A scared person should run to him for help because only he can protect and refresh us during hard times. 

We see how much effort sinners put into sinning—they try really hard and use clever ways to do wrong things. But we can be glad that they can’t cause more harm than God allows. 

Let’s try to free our hearts from selfish thoughts. People who are generous think about good things for God and ask Him to give them wisdom, understanding, and the joy of being close to Him. They hope to enjoy His glory someday.

32.o: When there was so much provocation given to the holy God, bad times might be expected. Alas! how many careless ones there are, who support self-indulgence by shameful niggardliness! We deserve to be deprived of the supports of life, when we make them the food of lusts. Let such tremble and be troubled. Blessed times shall be brought in by the pouring out of the Spirit from on high; then, and not till then, there will be good times. The present state of the Jews shall continue until a more abundant pouring out of the Spirit from on high. Peace and quietness shall be found in the way and work of righteousness. True satisfaction is to be had only in true religion. And real holiness is real happiness now, and shall be perfect happiness, that is, perfect holiness for ever. The good seed of the word shall be sown in all places, and be watered by Divine grace; and laborious, patient labourers shall be sent forth into God's husbandry.
32.m:
When God feels upset because people do wrong things, hard times could happen. Sadly, some people don’t care and waste money on selfish desires. People who misuse what they have should fear losing their blessings. 

Good times will come when God pours his Spirit onto everyone. Until then, things won't get better. Only those who follow God’s ways will find peace. Real joy comes from true faith. Being truly good means being happy now and forever.

God’s message will spread everywhere. He’ll send faithful workers to teach others about him.

33.o: Here we have the proud and false destroyer justly reckoned with for all his fraud and violence. The righteous God often pays sinners in their own coin. Those who by faith humbly wait for God, shall find him gracious to them; as the day, so let the strength be. If God leaves us to ourselves any morning, we are undone; we must every morning commit ourselves to him, and go forth in his strength to do the work of the day. When God arises, his enemies are scattered. True wisdom and knowledge lead to strength of salvation, which renders us stedfast in the ways of God; and true piety is the only treasure which can never be plundered or spent. The distress Jerusalem was brought into, is described. God's time to appear for his people, is, when all other helpers fail. Let all who hear what God has done, acknowledge that he can do every thing. Sinners in Zion will have much to answer for, above other sinners. And those that rebel against the commands of the word, cannot take its comforts in time of need. His wrath will burn those everlastingly who make themselves fuel for it. It is a fire that shall never be quenched, nor ever go out of itself; it is the wrath of an ever-living God preying on the conscience of a never-dying soul.
33.m:
Here we see the proud and deceitful enemy being punished fairly for all his lies and harm. A just God often gives people what they deserve. People who trust in God and wait patiently for him will find him kind. As each new day comes, our strength should match it. 

If God lets us face the day alone in the morning, we're lost. We need to give ourselves to God each morning and rely on his power to get through the day. When God stands up, his enemies run away.

True wisdom leads to real strength, helping us stay faithful to God’s way. True devotion is the only treasure that can’t be stolen or used up. 

The trouble that Jerusalem faced is explained here. God shows up for his people when everyone else fails. Everyone who hears about what God did should admit he can do anything. 

Sinners in the city of Zion will have more to account for than others. Those who disobey God’s commands can't enjoy his comfort when they’re in trouble. 

God’s anger burns forever on those who choose to fuel it. This rage is a fire that never goes out—it’s the eternal wrath of a living God burning inside a soul that lives forever.

33.o: The true believer watches against all occasions of sin. The Divine power will keep him safe, and his faith in that power will keep him easy. He shall want nothing needful for him. Every blessing of salvation is freely bestowed on all that ask with humble, believing prayer; and the believer is safe in time and for ever. Those that walk uprightly shall not only have bread given, and their water sure, but they shall, by faith, see the King of kings in his beauty, the beauty of holiness. The remembrance of the terror they were in, shall add to the pleasure of their deliverance. It is desirable to be quiet in our own houses, but much more so to be quiet in God's house; and in every age Christ will have a seed to serve him. Jerusalem had no large river running by it, but the presence and power of God make up all wants. We have all in God, all we need, or can desire. By faith we take Christ for our Prince and Saviour; he reigns over his redeemed people. All that refuse to have Him to reign over them, make shipwreck of their souls. Sickness is taken away in mercy, when the fruit of it is the taking away of sin. If iniquity be taken away, we have little reason to complain of outward affliction. This last verse leads our thoughts, not only to the most glorious state of the gospel church on earth, but to heaven, where no sickness or trouble can enter. He that blotteth out our transgressions, will heal our souls.
33.m:
The true believer stays alert to avoid any chance of sinning. God’s power keeps them safe, and trusting in that power makes them feel at ease. They won’t lack anything they need.
