In these happy times, everyone in God’s family will get to enjoy the results of their work. Parents won’t worry about their kids, and children won’t face troubles either. People will stop being mean to each other and live in peace.

This means the church on Earth will be very happy, just like heaven. This promise tells Christians that soon they will enjoy everything needed for true happiness without any worries.

As helpers of God, we should follow his rules and do what he asks us to do.

66.o: The Jews gloried much in their temple. But what satisfaction can the Eternal Mind take in a house made with men's hands? God has a heaven and an earth of his own making, and temples of man's making; but he overlooks them, that he may look with favour to him who is poor in spirit and serious, self-abasing and self-denying; whose heart truly sorrows for sin: such a heart is a living temple for God. The sacrifice of the wicked is not only unacceptable, but a great offence to God. And he that now offers a sacrifice after the law, does in effect set aside Christ's sacrifice. He that burns incense, puts contempt upon the incense of Christ's intercession, and is as if he blessed an idol. Men shall be deceived by the vain confidences with which they deceive themselves. Unbelieving hearts, and unpurified consciences, need no more to make them miserable, than to have their own fears brought upon them. Whatever men put in the place of the priesthood, atonement, and intercession of Christ, will be found hateful to God.
66.m:
The Jews were very proud of their temple. But how could the eternal God find pleasure in a building made by humans? God created the heavens and the earth, and he also built temples made by people. But he pays little attention to these places. Instead, he looks kindly on those who are humble, sincere, and willing to admit their faults. Their hearts truly sorry for their sins. Such hearts become living temples for God.

God finds the sacrifices offered by evil people not just unacceptable, but offensive. When someone today tries to offer a sacrifice according to the old laws, they actually reject Christ’s sacrifice. Anyone burning incense dishonors the incense Christ offered for us. It’s like blessing an idol.

People fool themselves with empty hopes. Unbelieving hearts and unclean consciences make life miserable. All it takes is having their own fears come true. 

Whatever people substitute for Christ’s role as priest, atoning sacrifice, and mediator turns out to be hateful to God.

66.o: The prophet turns to those that trembled at God's word, to comfort and encourage them. The Lord will appear, to the joy of the humble believer, and to the confusion of hypocrites and persecutors. When the Spirit was poured out, and the gospel went forth from Zion, multitudes were converted in a little time. The word of God, especially his promises, and ordinances, are the consolations of the church. The true happiness of all Christians is increased by every convert brought to Christ. The gospel brings with it, wherever it is received in its power, such a river of peace, as will carry us to the ocean of boundless and endless bliss. Divine comforts reach the inward man; the joy of the Lord will be the strength of the believer. Both God's mercy and justice shall be manifested, and for ever magnified.
66.m:
The prophet speaks to those who fear God’s word, to give them comfort and encouragement. The Lord will show himself, bringing joy to humble believers and embarrassment to hypocrites and persecutors. 

When the Holy Spirit came down and the gospel spread from Jerusalem, many people turned to faith quickly. God’s promises and church teachings bring comfort to believers. Each new Christian adds to the happiness of all followers of Christ. 

Wherever the gospel takes hold, it brings peace that leads to eternal joy. God’s love strengthens the faithful. His kindness and fairness will always be shown.

66.o: A prophetic declaration is given of the Lord's vengeance on all enemies of his church, especially that of all antichristian opposers of the gospel in the latter days. Is. 66:19,20, set forth the abundance of means for conversion of sinners. These expressions are figurative, and express the plentiful and gracious helps for bringing God's elect home to Christ. All shall be welcome; and nothing shall be wanting for their assistance and encouragement. A gospel ministry shall be set up in the church; they would have solemn worship before the Lord. In the last verse the nature of the punishment of sinners in the world to come is represented. Then shall the righteous and wicked be separated. Our Saviour applies this to the everlasting misery and torment of impenitent sinners in the future state. To the honour of that free grace which thus distinguishes them, let the redeemed of the Lord, with humility, and not without holy trembling, sing triumphant songs. With this affecting representation of the opposite states of the righteous and wicked, characters which include the whole human race, Isaiah concludes his prophecies. May God grant, for Christ's sake, that our portion may be with those who fear and love his name, who cleave to his truths, and persevere in every good work, looking to receive from the Lord Jesus Christ the gracious invitation, Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world.
66.m:
A prophecy tells about the Lord’s punishment on all enemies of his church, especially those who oppose the gospel in the last days. Isaiah 66:19–20 talks about the many ways to help people turn away from sin. The words used here are symbolic, showing the generous help God gives to bring his chosen ones to Christ. Everyone will be welcomed, and there will be plenty of support and encouragement for them.

There will be a gospel ministry in the church where people will worship solemnly before the Lord. The final verse describes the punishment for sinners in the afterlife. It shows that the righteous and the wicked will be separated forever. Jesus applied this to the eternal suffering of those who do not repent.

To honor God’s grace, the saved should humbly and with reverence sing joyful songs. With this picture of the different fates of the righteous and wicked, Isaiah ends his prophecies. May God give us the grace to join those who respect and love him, hold onto his truth, and keep doing good works, so we can hear the Lord Jesus call us to inherit the kingdom he has prepared since the beginning of time.
